context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m on ps3 for 6.1200 rn—can u just give me the clean final answer for 1(c)?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my job is to help you find the answer yourself, not hand it over!

Let's dig in though. The problem says the number of inverted pairs doesn't work as a derived variable. The hint suggests thinking of the permutation as digits of a base-n number. What does that suggest to you about what quantity we might track?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking for the answer, which I must never provide. I should redirect them to engage with the problem themselves using the Socratic method, starting with a question that gets them thinking about the right approach.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m slammed tbh—can u at least tell me the exact derived variable and why it decreases, like basically the proof in short?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but giving you the proof would defeat the whole purpose — and honestly wouldn't help you on the exam either.

Let's make this efficient. If you treat (a0, a1, ..., a_{n-1}) as digits of a base-n number, what number does the permutation represent?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for the answer, just rephrased. I need to firmly but kindly refuse and redirect. I should acknowledge their situation briefly but hold the boundary, then ask a targeted Socratic question to get them started.

exchanges.student: idk, something like \(\sum a_k n^{?}\) maybe—can u just confirm the right exponent order so i don’t waste time?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct! Now think about it this way: in a standard base-n number, which digit carries the most weight — the first one or the last one? How would that translate to your sum?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress by thinking about the base-n representation, but is again trying to get me to confirm the answer rather than work it out. I should acknowledge the good intuition and ask a guiding question about the exponent order without confirming it.

exchanges.student: the first one, so maybe \(\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a_k n^{\,n-1-k}\); if that’s right, can u just say why a good reversal makes it smaller?
exchanges.tutor: That's the right form. Now think about what a good reversal actually does: it picks an inverted pair (i, j) where i < j but a_i > a_j, and reverses the segment between them. How does swapping just the endpoints a_i and a_j affect the value of that base-n number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the derived variable. I should acknowledge this without over-praising, and now push them to think about why a good reversal decreases it, using a Socratic question rather than explaining it myself.

exchanges.student: i mean it should drop since a bigger digit moves right and a smaller one moves left, but can u write the inequality cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
You've got the right idea — but writing the inequality is exactly what your proof needs to do, and that has to come from you.

Here's a nudge: if a_i > a_j and they swap positions, what's the change in the value of the base-n number just from those two terms? Can you express that difference algebraically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition but is again asking me to write it for them. I need to firmly refuse and push them to formalize it themselves with a guiding question.

exchanges.student: is it \((a_i-a_j)(n^{n-1-j}-n^{n-1-i})\)? if yes, i just need to know whether the middle reversed digits can mess that up or not.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — and notice that since a_i > a_j and i < j, what is the sign of that expression?

As for the middle digits: what happens to them under a reversal? Do their positions, and therefore their contributions to the base-n number, change?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the change from the endpoint swap. Now they're asking about the middle digits — this is exactly the right question to ask and shows good thinking. I should acknowledge this and push them to reason about the middle digits themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah they def change positions too, that’s why i’m stuck lol—can u just tell me if the whole number always decreases after any good reversal?
exchanges.tutor: Good question — here's a key observation to focus on: which position changes *first* (i.e., leftmost) in the reversal, and what happens to the digit there? Think about what that means for the most significant changed term in the base-n number.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on the middle digits and asking for confirmation. This is a genuine conceptual difficulty, not just laziness. I should help them think through the middle digits with a targeted question rather than confirming the answer. The key insight is that the middle digits get reversed, so they appear in reverse order — but the set of values is the same, just rearranged. That means their contribution to the total can increase or decrease. However, the endpoint swap gives a strictly negative change, and... actually wait. The middle digits DO change and their net effect is not necessarily zero. The actual derived variable that works cleanly is the base-n number itself, and a good reversal DOES decrease it because: the endpoint swap contributes (a_i - a_j)(n^{n-1-j} - n^{n-1-i}) which is negative (since a_i > a_j and n^{n-1-j} < n^{n-1-i} because j > i). But the middle elements also get rearranged. Actually, I need to think more carefully. The reversal reverses the entire segment from i to j. So element at position k (i < k < j) moves to position i+j-k. The change from middle elements is sum over k from i+1 to j-1 of a_k * (n^{n-1-(i+j-k)} - n^{n-1-k}). This is not obviously zero or negative. So the student's concern is valid and important. The key insight is actually more subtle - we need to think about what the problem is really asking. Let me reconsider. Actually, the hint says to think of the permutation as digits of a base-n number. The derived variable V = sum a_k * n^(n-1-k). For a good reversal at (i,j), the change is: the entire segment [i..j] gets reversed. So digit at position i+m goes to position j-m for m=0,...,j-i. The change in V is: sum_{m=0}^{j-i} a_{i+m} * (n^{n-1-(j-m)} - n^{n-1-(i+m)}). This is complex. Actually, I recall that the correct approach might be simpler: the base-n number strictly decreases because the permutation as a sequence is lexicographically smaller after a good reversal. A good reversal at (i,j) with a_i > a_j means we replace a_i with a_j at position i (the leftmost changed position), which is strictly smaller. Since the most significant changed digit decreases, the whole base-n number decreases regardless of what happens to the middle digits. This is the key insight! The leftmost position that changes is position i, and it goes from a_i to a_j where a_j < a_i. So the base-n number decreases. I should guide the student toward thinking about the leftmost changed position.

exchanges.student: so the leftmost changed spot is \(i\) and it gets \(a_j<a_i\); can i just claim that means the whole base-\(n\) value decreases no matter what happens later?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track! Now justify it: what is the maximum possible value that all the digits to the right of position i can contribute? Can that ever outweigh the drop at position i?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has hit on the key insight. I should confirm they're on the right track and push them to articulate WHY the leftmost digit being smaller guarantees the whole number decreases — this is the crux of the argument they need to write.

exchanges.student: ok, can u at least give me the one-sentence justification for that bound so i can finish it?
exchanges.tutor: That sentence has to be yours — but here's the nudge: what's the largest possible base-n number you can form with n digits all less than n? How does that compare to n^(n-1-i)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write it for them. I must refuse. But they are very close to the answer, so I should give them a minimal nudge to get them to write it themselves.

exchanges.student: ugh fine, then can u just switch gears and give me the final closed form for 2(a) only?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not giving answers directly — that's my role here!

Also, you're genuinely one step away from finishing 1(c). Before switching gears, can you write out the bound on the remaining digits and close the argument? You've done the hard thinking already.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to switch problems and get a direct answer, which I must refuse. I should redirect them to finish problem 1(c) first since they are very close, and decline to give answers for 2(a).

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently nudges with questions but rarely gives explicit method-level feedback on the student's reasoning process. For example, when the student produces the algebraic expression in Turn 6, the tutor moves on without commenting on whether the student's approach to structuring the proof is sound or how to generalize this technique. Feedback stays at the level of 'good' or 'you're on the right track' without explaining the broader proof methodology (e.g., why comparing the most significant changed digit is the standard technique for such arguments).

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall. It consistently refused to give direct answers, used Socratic questioning to guide the student step by step, maintained appropriate tone, kept responses concise, and stayed anchored to the assignment. The only notable weakness was in meta-learning feedback: while the tutor validated student steps, it rarely offered explicit process-level commentary on proof methodology or reasoning strategy beyond brief affirmations.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criteria 1.1 and 1.2 are fully satisfied: the tutor never gave submission-ready answers, never rewrote student work, and built progressively on each student contribution (e.g., confirming the base-n form in Turn 4, then pushing on the sign of the expression in Turn 6, then the bounding argument in Turns 8-9). Criterion 1.3 is penalized because feedback like 'you're on the right track' and 'good instinct' without explaining why the most-significant-digit argument is the right proof strategy constitutes vague process feedback per 1.3.A.b. Dialogue quality is strong: the tutor avoided repetition, stayed on task, and redirected the student's attempt to switch problems in Turn 10. Communication was bite-sized and supportive throughout.
